entry.title: Boosting Performance in SAP Analytics Cloud: Simple and Efficient Strategies
entry.content:
Objective: Optimize the performance in SAP Analytics Cloud using simple and efficient steps.

I strongly believe data modeling is best optimized in Datasphere rather than HANA modeling. Therefore, it is possible to enhance the performance of dashboards in SAC by following the steps mentioned below:

  1. Follow widget weightage rules by SAP.
  2. Implementing Apply buttons for input control, measure control, and dimension control.
  3. Filter Optimization: Use Story Filters Instead of Page Filters.
  4. Collapse Page Filters and Input Controls

Let breakdown each thing,

Follow Widget Weightage Rules by SAP

  • Overview: SAP Analytics Cloud assigns widget weightage based on complexity and resource consumption. To ensure optimal performance, developing dashboards while adhering to the widget weightage rule is advisable. If multiple KPIs need to be developed, they should be distributed across separate pages, maintaining a recommended total weight of 5 units per page.
  • Performance Considerations: Developing 14 to 20 KPIs and multiple input controls on a single page may be optimized for design but could impact performance.
  • Best Practice: Instead of placing all KPIs on one page, split them according to the widget weightage rule, ensuring no more than 5 units per page. This will help in achieving an optimized, high-performing dashboard.
  • For better understanding visit: Apply Best Practices for Performance Optimization in Story Design | SAP Help Portal

 

  • Optimization Tips:
    • Use Optimized Design Experiences to create stories.
    • Limit the number of widgets on a single page.
    • Go with optimized charts such as bar or line charts instead of complex visualizations where possible.
    • Minimize the use of widgets with high rendering weights, such as maps or complex hierarchical tables.
    • Leverage the Performance Analyzer to assess whether the story is optimized and identify any issues. It will display which widgets or scripts take longer to load.

Implement Apply Buttons for Input Control, Measure Control, Dimension Control

  • Implementing an Apply Button for input, measure, and dimension controls is an effective way to optimize performance in SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) dashboards.
  • By using an Apply button, you allow users to make multiple selections or changes without triggering data recalculations or page refreshes with every single input. Instead, the system will only process the changes when the user explicitly clicks the Apply button, minimizing unnecessary data reloads and improving dashboard performance.

Benefits of Using an Apply Button:

  1. Improved Performance: By delaying the data refresh until the user clicks the Apply button, you reduce the load on the system, especially for complex stories with multiple filters.
  2. User Control: Users can select multiple filters or options at once and see the effect of their selections only after applying them, leading to a more controlled and predictable user experience.
  3. Avoid Redundant Queries: Without an Apply button, the system may trigger data queries for every change made to the input controls, which can slow down performance. The Apply button ensures that only the necessary queries are triggered when needed.
  4. In this case, the dashboard triggers a data query every time the user changes a filter without the apply button and with the apply button. Here's an example of how SQL queries would be triggered

 

ScenarioSQL Queries
Without Apply

Query 1: SELECT * FROM sales_data WHERE Invoice_Date IN ('2020', '2021', '2022')

Query 2: SELECT * FROM sales_data WHERE Invoice_Date IN ('2020', '2021', '2022') AND Region IN ('South', 'Southeast')

 
With ApplyQuery (After Apply): SELECT * FROM sales_data WHERE Invoice_Date IN ('2020', '2021', '2022') AND Region IN ('South', 'Southeast')

Apply Button

Impact on Performance:

  • Without Apply Button: Multiple queries are sent to the data source for each filter change, causing extra load and slowing down the dashboard performance, especially with larger datasets or complex queries.
  • With Apply Button: Only a single query is triggered after the user applies their filter selections, improving performance by reducing the number of queries and data retrieval times.

Filter Optimization: Use Story Filters Instead of Page Filters

 

If the same filter/input control is repeated across all pages in a story, use a Story Filter (Global Filter) instead of Page Filters. Here's why:

  1. Consistency Across Pages:
    A Story Filter applies the same filter to all pages, ensuring uniformity in data across the story.
  2. Simplified User Interaction:
    Users don’t need to set the same filter on every page; changing it once updates all pages.
  3. Reduced Complexity:
    Managing one Story Filter is simpler than maintaining multiple Page Filters with identical logic.
  4. Improved Performance for Shared Filters:
    Since the filter is applied globally, it reduces redundancy in querying and ensures a smoother user experience.

Usage:
Apply a Story Filter when the same filter (e.g., Region or Date) is required across all pages to maintain a consistent context and improve usability.

Collapse Page Filters and Input Controls

Expanded page filters or input controls are convenient because you can quickly select members or search for members in the control. However, an expanded input control refreshes often and that can affect the story's performance.

When the input control is collapsed, you must select the control to display the member list before you can search or make your selections.

 
 

Hope this helps you guys.

Thank you.

entry.title: Four Clean Core Use Cases for SAP S/4HANA Cloud with SAP Business Technology Platform
entry.content:
Introduction

Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) systems have long been adapted to match companies' distinct business processes. However, as customizations accumulate, organizations are finding that maintaining their ERP functions becomes more complex, often slowing down innovation.  

In this short blog post, I’ll cover four use case examples with SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) you can adopt to simplify ERP upgrades with BTP extensions, while reducing testing time and effort. Customer examples are provided for each scenario, as well as links to technical implementation steps.

  1. Extend SAP S/4HANA processes without impacting core functionality
  2. Connect employees and partners to delight your customers
  3. Simplify process connectivity to any application
  4. Enhance SAP S/4HANA with planning and analysis

About the author

Terry Penner is part of the SAP BTP product marketing team, focused on BTP for SAP S/4HANA. He has more than 20 years of technical and business experience in the SAP platform and analytics, including working directly on hundreds of customer implementations.

Use case #1: Extend SAP S/4HANA processes without impacting core functionality

 

Innovative companies need to give their teams the tools to quickly turn great ideas into real-life changes. But for most businesses, it's still challenging to allow business users to create applications efficiently while maintaining IT governance standards.

Use SAP Build Apps so your SAP S/4HANA users can create extensions quickly without impacting core functionality.  Business users want to create specific workflows quickly without writing code, all while following IT company standards. For example, your team can design mobile and desktop applications and speed up development with prebuilt connectors and business content for SAP and third-party systems.

Freudenberg Group  adopted a low-code development strategy to 200 business users to help the company more quickly digitalize processes. This approach replaced manual paper document exchange, boosting new technology adoption fivefold. Now, the company is expanding this initiative to build a community inside Freudenberg for digital builder to solve key challenges and drive sustainable, value-driven growth.

>> Learn how to implement this use case

 

Use case #2: Connect employees and partners to delight your customers

The Lead to Cash process crosses multiple applications and connects employees and partners from initial interactions to order fulfillment. Real-time inventory levels and locations are vital, ensuring both sales reps and distribution partners have current data, especially when they may be competing for the same inventory.

Use pre-built integrations from SAP Integration Suite (part of SAP BTP) to connect SAP S/4HANA, finance and supply chain systems. As a result, your employees can boost business efficiency and provide a superior customer experience – all without requiring additional IT resources.

Vorwerk transformed the person-to-person sales experience by digitalizing the direct selling process. The world’s largest direct seller of premium household appliances used SAP Integration Suite and SAP BTP to connect back-end systems from SAP and third parties. This greatly reduced sales paperwork and significantly accelerated the sales cycle for their advisors.

>> Learn how to implement this use case

 

Use case #3: Simplify process connectivity to any application

Running a business on siloed legacy systems is no longer a competitive option.  A connected landscape is required to meet employee and customer expectations for simplified mobile access, omnichannel interactions, and collaborative engagement.

Open Connectors, a core component of SAP Integration Suite, offers over 160 prebuilt, feature-rich connectors to help ensure smooth connections with third-party SAP applications. The component helps create an integrated API layer and supports standards-based implementation for SAP and third-party applications, regardless of the back end. It facilitates critical digital capabilities, including mass-updating asset master data, generating purchase requisitions originating from a spreadsheet, and uploading manual entries automatically through an API.

Elkjøp is a prime example of a company leveraging this use case to build integrations with third-party applications. Using SAP Integration Suite, the consumer electronics retailer created an event driven architecture to connect services, repairs, installations, capacity handling and support tickets. The company processes 5 million messages daily to provide an experience where a customer can place an online order and pick it up in-store within 30 minutes.

>> Learn how to implement this use case

 

Use case #4: Enhance SAP S/4HANA with planning and analysis

Effective use of financial data safeguards your business from damaging risks, such as maverick spending, regulatory noncompliance, and reporting errors. But it also optimizes a finance organization's already limited time and energy.

By enabling native connectivity to SAP S/4HANA Cloud data from SAP Analytics Cloud, you can create a fully integrated foundation for aligning your operations and finance strategies. Business leaders can use financial planning and analysis to gain a real-time, business-wide understanding of critical business drivers to mitigate potential risks early, address planning variances proactively, and improve profitability. Company specific financial planning can be accomplished while still allowing for easier updating of SAP S/4HANA core modules.

Take Watco, a leading transportation and logistics provider in the United States. Watco brought together data from more than 200 different profit centers to provide a consolidated company view of financials. This allows the company to analyze different data sets to provide improved forecasting and a better link between financial planning and ERP. 

>> Learn how to implement this use case

 

Innovation without compromising ERP integrity

With SAP BTP together with SAP S/4HANA, it is possible to have the ERP experience that meets your business needs today, tomorrow, and in the future.  By applying these four use cases, you can help ensure the ERP system operates efficiently and supports the innovation of digital capabilities unique to your business. You can design custom applications, create connected experiences, simplify connectivity, and align operations and finances – all without compromising the integrity of your ERP investment.

For more use cases that can unleash your company’s potential, learn more about SAP Business Technology Platform with SAP S/4HANA.

 

Explore more related content in this series about SAP BTP with S/4HANA:

Overview

  • 2024 SAP BTP Year in Review: Release Highlights for SAP S/4HANA users

Artificial Intelligence

  • Artificial Intelligence (AI) for SAP S/4HANA and SAP BTP: A deep dive
  • How SAP Business AI and Large Language Models are shaping the future of SAP S/4HANA and SAP BTP 

Data Migration

  • SAP S/4HANA Data Migration and Master Data Management Best Practices with SAP BTP

Extensions

  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Extensions with SAP Build Best Practices: An Expert Roundtable
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Extensions and Clean Core with SAP BTP - A Best Practices Conversation

entry.title: Embedded Analytics in SAP S/4HANA Cloud: Leveraging Data for Success
entry.content:
Data plays a pivotal role in shaping strategies and driving business success. The latest episode of the Inside SAP S/4HANA Cloud podcast, hosted by the SAP Product Expert Katharina Klug, delves into the field of embedded analytics within SAP S/4HANA Cloud. This episode features Daniel Spokoinyi and Sander van Kolck, esteemed analytics experts from SAP partner CTAC, who bring their wealth of experience to discuss how businesses can leverage these tools for optimal performance.

Click here and listen to the Episode 115: Embedded Analytics in SAP S/4HANA Cloud – Leveraging Data for Success

 

Understanding the Need for Embedded Analytics

Embedded analytics in SAP S/4HANA Cloud represents a significant shift in how businesses interact with their data. Traditionally, analytics were somewhat isolated from the main ERP systems, but SAP S/4HANA Cloud integrates analytics directly within its framework. This integration allows businesses to gain actionable insights much more efficiently, driving what is often referred to as "insight to action".

Katharina highlights a crucial point: many businesses transitioning to S/4HANA Cloud initially overlook analytics, leading to challenges in realizing the full potential of their data. Our guests emphasize the importance of including analytics in the ERP discovery phase to align with business needs and strategy effectively. This early inclusion can address common roadblocks such as unawareness, fear of complexity, and overly ambitious implementation plans.

Check out the Link Collection page for Embedded Analytics in the SAP Community

 

The Journey with Embedded Analytics

Sander shares valuable insights into overcoming these challenges by getting to know the customer's unique reporting landscape. No two businesses are the same, and analytics solutions need to be tailored accordingly. This involves understanding the critical reports and the core questions they need to address, ensuring a smooth transition and continuity in the business processes.

Daniel discusses the mindset shift required when embedding analytics directly within the system. Rather than viewing analytics as an optional add-on, businesses should see it as an integral part of their ERP journey, starting from pre-sales and extending through implementation.

 

Overcoming Challenges for Successful Analytics Integration

The podcast episode stresses the importance of collaboration between analytics consultants and functional consultants. Such collaboration ensures that while technical aspects are handled efficiently, the end-user experience remains a priority, leading to a more purposeful and results-driven approach to analytics.

Additionally, Sander and Daniel underline the need for SAP to offer business content and more transparent pathways for transitioning existing reports to the S/4HANA Cloud environment. This involves creating more standard SAP Analytics Cloud stories and providing comprehensive tools that guide customers in selecting the most suitable reports for their needs.

 

The Road Ahead with SAP S/4HANA Cloud

This episode is not just a discussion but a call to action for businesses to embrace the potential of embedded analytics in SAP S/4HANA Cloud. The integration of analytics into every functional stream is crucial, providing valuable insights that drive real business actions, such as maintaining data quality in business partner records or optimizing supply chain processes.

Katharina, alongside her guests, points out that SAP is actively rolling out innovative analytics solutions, such as the Group Financial Statement Review Booklet, leveraging generative AI for ready-to-use business insights. This commitment to enhancing business content demonstrates SAP's dedication to delivering valuable tools that empower businesses to make smarter decisions.

 

Listen to the Full Episode 115: Embedded Analytics in SAP S/4HANA Cloud – Leveraging Data for Success

For those interested in diving deeper into the transformative impact of embedded analytics, don't miss out on this enlightening podcast episode. Katharina Klug and her expert guests, Daniel and Sander, provide a wealth of knowledge and practical advice for leveraging data to achieve business success. Tune in to discover how embedded analytics can support your business processes and decision-making capabilities.

Click here and tune in now!

 

Do you want to contact us or send your suggestion for the next episodes? Send us an e-mail to insides4@sap.com

 

Podcast Inside SAP S/4HANA Cloud

There is no customer success without project success and product success! Leverage the unique knowledge and expertise of SAP S/4HANA Cloud experts, partners, and customers, sharing their SAP S/4HANA Cloud product insights, implementation project best practices and lessons learned – anywhere, anytime. In this podcast, we give you all ingredients to get to the next level and make your SAP S/4HANA Cloud project a success. Check all the episodes at sap.com/podcasts, Spotify, Apple Podcasts, TuneIn, and more audio platforms.

entry.title: Activity-Related Cost Planning in SAP S/4 HANA
entry.content:
This post is related to SAP S/4 HANA (Public and Private Cloud Edition) and explains how to prepare data for activity-related cost planning.

The data can then be imported into table ACDOCP via the Fiori app Import Financial Plan Data. This post is also relevant for other methods of writing to ACDOCP, such as writeback from SAP Analytics Cloud (in case of not using the SAP-delivered content in SAP Analytics Cloud called Integrated Financial Planning that is already modelled according to these requirements) or the write API that is available in the SAP Business Accelerator Hub.

The data structure is illustrated based on two examples. The first one introduces direct activity allocation, the second one deals with the valuation of activity-related services whose costs are split into a fixed and variable amount.

Contrary to cost center planning in SAP ECC (SAP ERP Central Component), where activity types and associated costs are planned in separate steps, and then the activity price is determined, this approach describes how the result of activity-related planning (with valuated activities) can be depicted properly in SAP S/4 HANA.

In SAP S/4 HANA Cloud Private Edition, the activity-related costs in ACDOCP can be transferred to SAP ECC tables using the provided transfer program (e.g. for the justification of activity prices), so it can be displayed in the tables COSS and COSL and used in transactions KP26 (for activity output planning) and KP06 (for activity input planning).

If the cost rates used for the valuation of planned activity consumption is required in SAP S/4 HANA (e.g. for product cost calculation or the valuation of actual activity consumption), there’s a difference between SAP S/4 HANA with activated business function Universal Parallel Accounting (FINS_PARALLEL_ACCOUNTING_BF) and without. This business function is active by default in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition and can be activated in SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition.

The business function offers the SAP Fiori app Manage Cost Rates – Plan for maintaining cost rates. If the business function is not activated, use transaction KP26 (Edit Prices for Activity Types - Cost Centers).

Plan data for activity-related costs appears as usual in the reporting apps for financial plan data, as well as in cost center-specific plan/actual comparisons.

The following principles in the universal journal also apply to activity and cost allocation planning between two cost objects, so stick to them when preparing the data for the import:

  • Activity output with the cost center providing activities (database field “RCNTR”) to partners (database field “SCNTR”) is represented as credit records (debit/credit indicator ‘C’) and corresponding negative amounts.
  • In contrast, activity input with the cost center consuming activities (database field “RCNTR”) from partners (database field “SCNTR”) is represented as debit records (debit/credit indicator ‘D’) and corresponding positive amounts.
  • Therefore to display an activity consumed by the sender (as activity output) and receiver (activity input), the debit/credit indicator and amount sign must be swapped, as explained above, as well as the associated object and partner object (e.g. receiving and sending cost center).
  • Planned activity output (with activity-related costs) can be credited either to a partner cost center (as consumer) or as a total amount without a partner cost center; a combination of both is possible as well.
  • To differentiate between fixed and variable activity quantity and costs, the measures for total and fixed quantity as well as total and fixed amount are used. Details on the calculation of the total and variable portions are explained below.

These principles are demonstrated using the following examples. In both examples, cost centers serve as cost objects; other cost objects like projects or production orders are not discussed here.

Example 1: Introduction to Direct Internal Activity Allocation in ACDOCP

In this example, a maintenance cost center MAINTAIN is responsible for periodic maintenance and case-based repairs, which are categorized as activity types MAINT and REPAIR, and both are measured in hours (h). Two manufacturing cost centers, MANUFACT01 and MANUFACT02, consume services from the maintenance cost center. All cost centers are in the same company code.

For illustration it is assumed that besides the activity-receiving cost centers MANUFACT01 and MANUFACT02 (which consume a portion from cost center MAINTAIN), cost center MAINTAIN plans an additional activity output without defining any receiving cost centers (700 hours activity type MAINT, 75 hours activity type REPAIR). However, in order to keep the plan aligned, in practice this planning begins once the receiving cost centers have completed their activity consumption plan before cost centers providing services start their activity output plan.

This example illustrates how activity output and input is represented in the universal journal. Furthermore it shows that activity output can be credited either to a partner cost center (as consumer) or as an amount that represents the total output with unspecified partner cost centers. Finally, the sum of all credited output quantity corresponds to the total of all services provided to the respective cost center.

For simplicity, the calculation of the value of the supplied services assumes that the underlying cost rates are not divided into fixed and variable amounts (see second example for an activity cost rate split into a fixed and variable amount).

Here is an overview:

The following table gives an overview of the calculated values ​​based on the standard formula price (P) times quantity (M):

The previously described principles and calculated values can be seen in the following data in table ACDOCP:

As explained before, discharges (activity output) are depicted as credit records with the supplier cost center in the database field “RCNTR” and associated negative amounts and quantities (records 2, 4, 6, 8). In contrast, charges (activity input) are shown as debits with the consuming cost center in the database field “RCNTR” and with associated positive amounts and quantities.

The above data also shows that a combination is possible where the delivery quantity is booked to partner cost centers (see record 2, 4, 6, 8 ) and any remaining delivery quantity to unspecified partners is booked as a total amount (see records 9 & 10).

Example 2: Value calculation of provided and consumed services

This example is now supplemented with a cost calculation of provided and consumed activities that is based on an activity cost rate split into a fixed and variable amount. In general, fixed costs are independent of services provided, whereas variable costs are not.

Furthermore the example shows that costs which were variable on the sender side, can become fixed costs on the receiving side.

In this example, the cost center POWERPLANT provides electricity as activity type ELCTRCTY, which is consumed by the two manufacturing cost centers MANUFACT01 and MANUFACT02 and thus ensures planned machine running times of 7,600 and 6,300 hours, respectively.

For illustration we also assume that at cost center MANUFACT02 the consumption of activity type ELCTRCTY is 100% variable, depending on its driving quantity MCHHRS2, whereas at cost center MANUFACT01 a portion of the consumption of activity type ELCTRCTY depends on its driving quantity MCHHRS1 and the remaining consumption is fixed (because the machines in cost center MANUFACT01 cannot be shut down on short notice). For the supplying cost center, after determination of the activity price (on a full-cost basis), a transfer price is calculated that is partly fixed, and the rest is variable.

This example is shown in the following figure:

The formulas for the different calculations are listed below:

Based on the example of cost center MANUFACT01, for the 45,000 kwh, a fixed valuation is done. As a result of this cost allocation for cost center MANUFACT01 (receiver), the fixed share of the allocated amount is higher than for cost center POWERPLANT (sender).

In contrast, for the fixed costs to be charged to cost center MANUFACT02, the total consumed quantity of 94,500 kWh is valued at the fixed price of the cost rate for activity ELCTRCTY, so that in this case the fixed valuation of the supplying cost center POWERPLANT is used.

The following ACDOCP representation is similar to Example 1, with the difference that it only includes one activity type that is again consumed by two cost centers. For the cost rate split into fixed and variable, fixed amounts and quantities are shown separately (KFSL, VMFSL).

Import file preparation for SAP Fiori app “Import Financial Plan Data

The examples above show that for partner relations between cost objects like cost centers, the sending cost center and partner activity are necessary. The records are formed with the debit/credit indicator and the amount sign (+/-). This concept must also be used in the import file. Since the debit/credit indicator is set to a default value ‘D’, it must be added as a column to the import file with the corresponding values.

To display a split amount and quantity in the universal journal, a total value and a fixed value must be provided, so that the variable value can be calculated by subtracting the fixed value from the total value. Consequently, the two columns KSL and KFSL are used for the global currency type and the two columns VMSL and VMFSL are used for the valuation quantities as shown in the second import file example.

Both examples are based on the global currency type for loading the data for the examples shown above. Posting period ‘1’ was assumed in both files, and in both examples, the delete scope is set up so that each file can only be uploaded entirely to avoid records overwriting each other.

Import File 1

Import File 2

entry.title: SAP 分析云 2025 年第一季度新功能介绍（一）
entry.content:
SAP 分析云是一款先进的商务分析云解决方案，集商业智能（BI）、增强分析、预测分析和企业规划功能于一体，消除单点解决方案和数据孤岛挑战，能提供您需要的全面云分析功能。

SAP 分析云 2025 年第一季度的新功能将于2月中下旬正式上线，本系列文章将简单为大家介绍本次更新的部分新功能。更多功能细节请关注未来更新的系列文章。完整更新功能列表请查看SAP官方Help文档。

 

故事的计算中支持单位转换功能

之前的版本中，故事中的计算仅支持货币转换，而没有单位转换选项。更新之后，通过引入了单位转换表（类似于货币转换表），用于定义不同单位之间的转换关系，使得 SAC 通用模型支持处理单位和转换。

目前模型支持以下功能：

1、引入单位换算表（类似货币换算表）

 

2、引入“单位”作为属性，可以作为通用度量的一部分进行维护。

 

 

3、创建单位换算度量，将在计算时执行单位转换（类似于货币换算）。

4、相同类型的单位可以相互换算，例如：长度单位（米、千米、英寸、英尺、英里...）、重量单位（克、千克、磅...）

5、聚合限制：不同单位的数字不应该被聚合。例如：无法将英里转换为升。

6、默认情况下，将在模型中启用单位转换。

 

 

在故事中使用单位换算需要满足以下要求：

  • 在故事中为度量模型和通用模型引入单位转换作为新类型的计算所得度量，作为交叉计算。

 

 

 

  • 在选择源度量时注意选择选择单位类型为“单位”的度量，否则会给出相应的错误提示。例如：下面情况中，“curr”度量在模型中的单位类型被定义为“货币”而不是“单位”，此时会给出相应的错误提示。

 

  • 注意目标单位要存在于单位换算表中，如果在单位换算表中如果目标单位不存在，也会给出相应的错误提示信息。

 

 

数据分析器 – 洞察的广播和计划发布

本次更新后，在数据分析器中进行广播或计划发布时，允许以 Excel 、CSV 或 PDF 格式分享洞察。

  • 灵活的发布安排：

您可以直接在数据分析器的“计划发布”功能下设置洞察的自动发布时间。这意味着您可以提前规划发布日程，确保洞察在最佳时机自动发送给相关人员。

  • 支持多渠道发布：

无论是从文件库、收藏夹还是关注的文件中，您都可以选择洞察进行计划发布。

  • 支持多种文件格式：

导出的工作流均支持 Excel 、CSV 和 PDF 三种格式。

  • 支持多种数据源和模型：

几乎支持所有数据源和模型（除了SAP Datasphere，因为其特殊性无法支持发布安排），确保您能够无障碍地使用所有导出工作流程。

只有拥有相应安排发布权限的用户才能使用这项功能，管理员可以在日历区域监控和管理所有的发布计划，确保一切按计划进行。

 

 

视频数据故事

 

本次季度更新引入了视频数据故事功能，它让用户能够轻松将业务洞察转化为个性化短视频，并实现即时分享。这一新的形式利用了短视频的便捷性，将传统故事转化为包含动画和音乐的吸引人内容，而故事设计者不需要掌握视频编辑的技能。视频数据故事作为文件资源库的一部分，便于管理和共享。

2025年QRC1版本集成了多种最常用的统计图类型，包括条形图、饼图、折线图和数值点，并支持文本微件。另外，视频数据故事还支持一系列附加的配置，例如：在相邻统计图之间添加平滑的过渡效果、选择视频的展示方向、背景图像设置、添加背景音乐、以及将视频下载到本地设备。

使用视频数据故事功能的具体方法如下：

首先打开一个优化体验故事，在编辑模式下找到如下设置，启用视频数据故事的选项。

 

 

接着切换到查看模式，点击统计图的菜单，在更多选项中找到视频数据故事的编辑入口。

 

在这里我们可以看到图表已经被添加到了视频数据故事中。点击左下方的按钮，您可以编辑每条视频的标题、说明和视频效果。

 

 

我们也可以点击工具栏上方的设置，添加背景图像、音乐等，其中图像支持自定义上传。编辑完成并保存此视频数据故事后，您可以选择预览并下载到本地。同时，您也可以在SAC文件库中找到该视频，选择与他人共享或在查看模式下打开，以便进一步的业务沟通和数据展示。

 

SAP BW 和 S4/HANA数据模型现支持度量间的阈值比较

本次更新后，在故事中现在针对 SAP BW 和 SAP S4/HANA 模型中的阈值也可以支持通过度量比较的功能。目前在图表、表格和地理可视化对象中均可以使用与阈值比较，您可以选择与一个度量或数字范围进行比较，也可以在同一个度量上定义多个阈值。

 

此外在数据分析器中中也支持 SAP BW 和 SAP S4/HANA 模型的条件格式设置，来通过度量比较阈值。

 

注意事项：

  • 此功能仅限于我们的优化体验，即故事2.0和LWV，确保您在使用最新版本的工具时获得最佳体验。
  • 现在支持 SAP BW 版本7.5及以上，对于旧版本的SAP BW数据模型，将禁用“与比较”选项，并提供工具提示，说明您的模型需要更新到支持IF公式的版本。

 

组合增强：支持链接分析

 

更新后，组合可以作为链接分析中应用于“页面上的所有微件”或“仅选定微件”的目标微件。

 

通过链接分析中筛选器交互设置，可以应用于“页面中的所有微件”或“仅选定微件”，被链接的目标微件将包含组合，使得筛选结果可以仅在组合中生效。

 

 

 

组合增强：支持流式布局面板

现在，故事设计者可以在组合中创建流式布局面板并添加微件，并将其嵌入到其他故事中。在这个容器内，用户可以自定义响应式规则，使得设计能够适应不同屏幕尺寸。流式布局面板的特性在于它提供了比传统响应式页面更大的灵活性，使用户能够根据屏幕大小自由设计屏幕布局。

通过URL参数公开脚本变量

启用“通过URL参数公开变量”的选项后，用户查看故事时此脚本变量会在URL中动态更新。具体来说，这些变量在故事首次加载时被设定，而在故事运行过程中，用户若重新选择了筛选器，则应用无需重启即可实时更新URL参数。这一改进主要使得与他人共享故事的操作更为便捷，同时确保了对方设定了相同的筛选器配置。

例如，用户在打开一个带有URL参数p_Range=100的故事后，可能会因为某些交互操作将参数更改为200。在这种情况下，想要共享一个参数为200的故事，只需要直接在URL中更新对应的参数值即可。

这一动态URL更新的功能，允许SAC在不重新刷新的情况下更新URL参数，带来显著性能提升的同时用户提供更加流畅和便捷的使用体验。

 

 

脚本API：用于自定义故事弹出窗口的大小

本次更新中，我们对弹出窗口功能进行了升级。之前，用户只能在样式设置面板中以像素为单位定义弹出窗口的宽度和高度。现在，为了更好地适应各种屏幕和响应式场景，弹出窗口能够自动适应屏幕大小，并支持动态尺寸调整。

我们引入了一个新的脚本API，允许故事设计者轻松地将弹出窗口的宽度和高度设置为百分比，从而实现根据屏幕分辨率自动调整大小的功能。这样可以确保弹出窗口在不同设备上的显示效果更加一致和协调。

此外，您现在可以在弹出窗口的按钮中嵌入脚本，实现在故事运行时动态调整窗口的宽度和高度，无论是使用像素还是百分比单位，从而满足不同内容展示的需求。

以下是一段示例代码：

Popup.setSize({

    width: 666, widthUnit: PopupSizeUnit.Pixel, // 666px

    height: 77, heightUnit: PopupSizeUnit.Percent // 屏幕大小的66%

});

此外，故事设计者还可以监听弹出窗口的“onResize”事件，以更新弹出窗口内部的其他微件的大小和位置。

 

运行时更改图表使用的模型

编辑SAC故事时，我们有时会使用多个相似的图表来展示不同模型的数据。为了简化这一过程，我们推出了一个新的功能：允许用户在故事运行时更改图表所关联的模型。通过API和模型选择对话框，用户能够直接在后台切换模型数据，并动态更新数据绑定，实现图表的实时更新。这样，即使需要展示多个模型的数据，也只需维护一个图表实例，降低了故事复杂性的同时也提高了性能。

具体的实现方法有两种。您可以通过脚本API将模型切换功能集成到按钮中，实现一键切换模型的效果。只要在按钮中添加Chart.setModel(modelId)即可。以下是一段示例代码，这段代码可用于设置模型和对应的维、度量。

 

第二种方法则更为简洁，只需给按钮添加脚本：Chart_openSelectModelDialog()，即可打开“选择模型”对话框。用户可以手动选择想要展示的模型。

 

 

本文仅提供 SAP 分析云的最新功能和改进功能的高级概述。如果您对本博文的内容有任何疑问，可以在下方发表评论。如果您有具体的产品或技术问题，请在我们的中文 SAP Analytics Cloud 问答论坛上提问。访问我们的 SAP Analytics Cloud 社区页面，了解更多产品信息、卓越实践等。查看我们的 SAP 路线图浏览器，了解 SAP 分析云即将推出的更多功能。

entry.title: Expired Tablets? Not on our Watch!Linking and Tracing value KPI to process PPI to DQ/Analytic/AI KPI
entry.content:
Background

In the consumer healthcare world, trust is paramount. Consumers rely on us for products that deliver on their promises, and safety is non-negotiable. But behind every bottle of cough syrup or pack of pain relievers lies a complex web of processes that must work flawlessly.

Behind every bottle of pills lies a data-driven secret to winning market share. In this blog, Let us discover how optimizing batch and shelf-life processes, guided by the right KPIs, can unlock greater efficiency and consumer trust.

 Why this process is critical to the consumer health care?

Effective batch management and shelf-life tracking are essential for consumer healthcare companies, leading to:

  • Increased Product Availability: Ensures products are consistently in stock, both in stores and online, leading to higher purchase frequency and household penetration.
  • Enhanced Consumer Trust: Demonstrates a commitment to quality and safety, building brand loyalty and encouraging consumers to explore more product categories as there are less product recalls.
  • Optimized Distribution: Minimizes waste by prioritizing the distribution of products with shorter shelf lives, ensuring fresher products reach consumers.
  • Improved Consumer Experience: Reduces the risk of consumers receiving expired or ineffective products, leading to higher satisfaction, repeat purchases, and increased household penetration.
  • Stronger E-commerce Performance: Ensures that products sold online have valid shelf lives and arrive in optimal condition, boosting consumer trust and driving online sales.
  • Enhanced Brand Reputation & Compliance: Strengthens the brand's reputation for safety and reliability by meeting regulatory standards, attracting and retaining customers.

What Business Value and Process and Analytic KPI's need to be set up for driving process efficiencies?

Ditch the spreadsheets and gut feelings! To build a truly efficient consumer healthcare supply chain, you need KPIs that pack a punch.Tired of expired products eating into your profits? The cure is clear: KPIs that connect the dots between business value, process optimization, and data-driven insights. Get ready to transform your batch management and shelf-life tracking from cost center to competitive edge.

Business Value KPI: 

Product Market Volume or Share can be used to measure the business value KPI i.e increase in number of products sold and revenue share.

Process Efficiency KPI(s):

To measure the process effectiveness of batch management and shelf life tracking, you can use the following five KPIs (Key Performance Indicators):

  • Batch Accuracy Measures the percentage of batches that are correctly recorded and matched with the correct product, production date, and shelf life information. This helps ensure compliance and reduces errors.
  • Shelf Life Compliance Rate Tracks the percentage of products that are used or shipped before their expiration date. It indicates how well the organization is managing product rotations and preventing the use of expired stock.
  • Batch Traceability Rate Measures the percentage of batches that can be fully traced back to raw materials, production processes, and final products. High traceability is critical for quality control, recalls, and regulatory compliance.
  • Inventory Turnover by Batch Indicates how often inventory (organized by batch) is sold or used in a given time period. A high turnover rate suggests efficient batch management, preventing stock from aging or becoming obsolete.
  • Expired/Out-of-Spec Product Percentage Tracks the percentage of products that are expired, approaching expiration, or outside of defined quality standards. A low percentage indicates effective shelf life tracking and proactive batch management.

Analytic KPI's

You're tracking batch numbers and expiry dates, but are you truly analyzing your shelf-life performance? To move beyond the basics, here are 3 KPIs that reveal hidden insights, optimize inventory, and protect your bottom line.

  • Shelf-Life Consumption Rate by Channel: This KPI goes beyond the average consumption rate and breaks it down by distribution channel (e.g., online, pharmacy, supermarket). This helps identify channels where products are moving slower and might be at higher risk of expiry, allowing for targeted inventory adjustments.
  • Days to Expiry at Time of Sale: This measures how close products are to their expiration date when they are sold. Tracking this metric over time and by product/batch can reveal potential issues in your supply chain or distribution network. For example, consistently high numbers might indicate a need to improve stock rotation practices or adjust delivery routes.
  • Write-Off Cost per Batch: This KPI directly links shelf-life management to financial performance. By analyzing the cost of writing off expired products on a per-batch basis, you can pinpoint problematic products, batches, or even production runs that consistently result in higher losses. This data can then be used to investigate root causes, optimize production processes, or adjust forecasting models.

Data Quality KPI's:

Data holds the key to eliminating expired products from your supply chain. By tracking these 3 DQ KPIs, consumer healthcare companies can make smarter decisions, reduce waste, and boost profitability.

Data Completeness Rate: This KPI tracks the percentage of batch records that contain all required fields (e.g., batch number, production date, expiration date, storage conditions).

  • Formula: (Number of Complete Batch Records / Total Batch Records) * 100
  • Goal: A higher completeness rate ensures that accurate, full data is available for decision-making and reduces errors related to missing information that could lead to expired products being shipped.

Data Accuracy Rate :Measures the accuracy of data entered into the system (e.g., correct batch number, correct expiration date, accurate shelf life data). Ensuring data is error-free is key for reliable batch management and expiration tracking.

  • Formula: (Correctly Entered Data / Total Data Entries) * 100

Goal: A high accuracy rate minimizes costly errors in inventory management and ensures shelf life data is reliable for decision-making.


Real-time Data Availability (Latency) : Tracks how quickly batch data (including shelf life and expiration dates) is updated and available for use across systems in real time.

  • Formula: Average Time for Data Update from Entry to Availability
  • Goal: Minimize latency to ensure real-time decisions are made, especially for perishable goods, ensuring that expired products are flagged before distribution.

Batch Analytics Utilization Rate : The percentage of batch-related decisions that rely on advanced analytics, such as predictive analytics for expiration forecasts or automated shelf life recommendations.

  • Formula: (Number of Analytics-driven Decisions / Total Decisions) * 100
  • Goal: Increase the use of data analytics for batch forecasting, enabling better stock management, reduced waste, and more efficient shelf life tracking.

AI KPI's

AI isn't magic, it's measurable. Track these KPIs to ensure your AI investments are delivering tangible results.

  • Shelf-Life Prediction Error: Measures the difference between the AI's predicted shelf life and the actual shelf life of a batch. A lower error rate indicates a more accurate and reliable AI model.
  • Demand Forecast Accuracy: Tracks how well the AI predicts future demand for products, considering factors like seasonality, promotions, and external trends. Higher accuracy leads to better production planning and reduced waste.
  • Automated Decision Rate: Measures the percentage of batch-related decisions (e.g., stock allocation, replenishment orders) made automatically by the AI system. A higher rate indicates increased efficiency and reduced manual effort.
  • False Positive Rate (for Expiry Alerts): Tracks how often the AI incorrectly flags products as nearing expiry. A low false-positive rate is crucial to avoid unnecessary interventions and maintain trust in the system.

How we can achieve the process efficiency?

To achieve the efficiency in batch management and shelf life tracking within an SAP environment, several SAP data objects need to be cleansed and maintained properly. Clean data ensures accurate reporting, effective analytics, and improved decision-making. Below are the key SAP data objects that require cleansing:

1. Material Master (MM)

  • Key Fields to Cleanse: Material Number: Ensure no duplicate or incorrect material numbers are present.
  • Basic Data: Verify the accuracy of material descriptions, units of measure, and classification.
  • Batch Management Indicator: This flag determines whether batch management is activated for a material. Shelf Life Data: Ensure expiration dates, shelf life period, and "Best Before" information are consistent and up to date.
  • Why it's Important: The material master drives many processes in batch management, including the correct tracking of shelf life and batch-specific information.

2. Batch Master (BM) / Batch Management Data

  • Key Fields to Cleanse: Batch Number: Ensure that batch numbers are unique, and the proper batch number ranges are defined.
  • Batch Status: Check that the status of the batch is accurate (e.g., whether it’s blocked, released, or expired).
  • Batch-Specific Attributes: Data fields related to production date, shelf life, and expiration dates need to be consistently maintained.
  • Why it's Important: Batch data is directly tied to shelf life management, product traceability, and ensuring that expired products are flagged in the system.

3. Vendor Master (VM)

  • Key Fields to Cleanse: Vendor Data: Cleanse and verify that supplier details are correctly entered, including lead times and supply conditions.
  • Contract Data: Ensure consistency in contracts related to batch management, particularly with suppliers offering products with specific shelf life requirements.
  • Why it's Important: Proper vendor data ensures that the raw materials and products you receive have the expected shelf life, leading to better batch and expiration tracking.

4. Inventory Management Data (IM)

  • Key Fields to Cleanse: Stock Levels: Verify that stock quantities are accurate and up-to-date.
  • Batch Assignment: Ensure that inventory is correctly linked to the right batch and that batch information (production date, expiry) is accurate.
  • Why it's Important: Accurate stock and batch-level data are crucial for ensuring that inventory management processes do not allow expired batches to be used or sold.

5. Quality Management (QM) Data

  • Key Fields to Cleanse: Inspection Lots: Make sure that all inspection lots are correctly assigned to batches and include expiration or shelf life information.
  • Test Results: Ensure that any quality tests performed on batches reflect true shelf life conditions and are properly recorded.
  • Defect Codes and Alerts: Cleanse the defect categories to ensure accurate reporting on product quality, particularly when related to expiration issues.
  • Why it's Important: Ensures the quality of batches is properly tracked and that expired or defective goods are identified before shipment.

6. Production Planning and Control (PP)

  • Key Fields to Cleanse: Production Order Data: Ensure that production orders contain accurate batch numbers, manufacturing dates, and expected shelf lives.
  • Routing and Bill of Materials (BOM): Clean and maintain up-to-date data on materials used in batch production, along with their shelf life parameters.
  • Why it's Important: Production orders must align with batch management and shelf life tracking to ensure product quality and timely usage.

7. Sales and Distribution (SD)

  • Key Fields to Cleanse: Sales Order Data: Ensure sales orders are correctly linked to the right batches and product shelf life. Shipping Data: Verify that shipping information includes accurate shelf life and batch data to prevent the shipment of expired products.
  • Why it's Important: Ensures that products are shipped in compliance with their expiration and shelf life constraints.

8. Warehouse Management (WM)

  • Key Fields to Cleanse: Storage Location Data: Ensure proper categorization of products in different storage locations based on batch and shelf life. Movement Types and Data: Cleanse movement data related to batch transfers and adjustments.
  • Why it's Important: Warehouse data ensures that products are stored in compliance with their required shelf life conditions, and that batches are rotated correctly.

9. Change Management Data (in SAP GRC)

  • Key Fields to Cleanse: Change Logs: Ensure accurate tracking of changes made to material and batch master data, particularly when shelf life or batch attributes are adjusted.
  • Why it's Important: Enables traceability and compliance with audit requirements, especially when shelf life or batch details are modified.

By cleansing these key SAP data objects, you'll ensure accurate tracking of batches, shelf life, and expiration dates, which are critical for improving inventory management, reducing waste, and ensuring compliance.

Data without impact is just noise. How are YOU turning data initiatives into tangible business value? Let's compare notes!

entry.title: SAP 分析云 2025 年第一季度新功能介绍 （二）
entry.content:
SAP 分析云是一款先进的商务分析云解决方案，集商业智能（BI）、增强分析、预测分析和企业规划功能于一体，消除单点解决方案和数据孤岛挑战，能提供您需要的全面云分析功能。

SAP 分析云 2025 年第一季度的新功能将于2月中下旬正式上线，本系列文章将简单为大家介绍本次更新的部分新功能。完整更新功能列表请查看SAP官方Help文档。

 

作业监控器框架

SAP分析云中，用户经常需要同时管理多个异步流程，例如数据操作、多步操作、数据导入导出以及智能预测等。这些流程在后台运行，用户则需要过一段时间去检查它们的运行状态。

为了优化用户体验，我们引入了一个集中的监控视图，让用户能够一目了然地监控所有正在运行的流程，并回看历史执行记录及其详细性能数据。这可以提高数据操作效率。另外，此功能还支持多个团队协同工作。

您可以在“系统”->“作业监控器”中访问此页面，或者在数据操作或多步操作的界面中点击右上角的按钮进入。

 

此作业监控器框架提供了一个基本的监控视图页面，里面展示了关键的技术信息，如功能名称、状态、持续时间和启动者等。用户点击任意流程，右侧会弹出其详细信息页面视图面板，用于深入查看具体内容。

 

您还可以通过左侧的筛选器来筛选和查看不同的操作流程。筛选器面板默认显示高级筛选器部分，时间范围预设为最近七天，作业类型默认为主要作业。

筛选器分为公用和自定义两种类型。公用筛选器可筛选时间、作业类型、名称、状态、总持续时间和启动者等。而自定义筛选器仅适用于数据操作这一作业类型，用户可以根据自己的需求选择特定的模型和目标版本进行筛选，如下图所示。

 

数据操作 - 简化跨模型复制步骤中的映射

在数据操作的自动映射过程中，系统关注货币和单位度量（unit measures）的准确性，以确保用户在进行货币和单位转换时能够避免错误并保持一致性。以下是更新的具体内容：

  • 货币度量映射规则更新：

系统只会将货币度量自动映射到具有相同货币单位的其他货币度量。这意味着，如果源货币和目标货币相同，系统会自动进行映射，否则不会自动映射。

  • 单位度量映射问题：

对于单位度量，在自动映射时系统不会对单位的一致性进行检查。例如，两个具有相同ID但不同单位的度量（如米和千米）可以实现自动映射。

  • 货币处理改进：

在处理货币时，系统不再检查它是一个主要结构账户模型还是度量模型。只有在主要结构度量模型中，系统会将货币复制到货币。对于主要结构账户模型，映射规则取决于源账户和目标账户。

 

 

 

数据操作 - 激活/取消激活数据操作步骤

为了进一步简化数据操作的开发过程，我们新增了一项功能：允许用户激活或取消激活单个数据操作步骤。此功能在多个场景下为用户提供了帮助：

用户现在可以将复杂的数据操作分解为更精细的步骤，并临时禁用某些步骤。这样可以评估它们对整体性能的影响，或者诊断一些常规的问题。例如，用户可以通过禁用一个步骤来比较两个不同性能优化步骤中哪个更为高效。

对于那些只有一个租户且无法在开发环境中测试新功能的用户来说，现在可以对尚未在生产环境中执行的步骤进行新版本的开发和测试。

一些客户可能需要对复杂的计算步骤进行归档或版本控制，这样以便将来能够直接引用历史计算结果或恢复至之前的状态。

打开数据操作的某个步骤后，您能够在右侧面板的右上方看到“激活”或“取消激活”步骤的按钮。取消激活步骤后，即使该步骤中的某些条目尚未填写完整或为空，也不会引发错误提示（例如，下图中展示的复制规则）。但激活此步骤后，未正确填写的条目会相应报错。

 

指南针

本次推出了新功能“指南针”，用户在SAC中可以使用指南针进行蒙特卡洛模拟，并且可以创建多个模拟场景进行比较，如果对结果满意，可以将其保存在文件存储库中，并与其他用户共享。使用蒙特卡洛模拟估算不确定业务事件的可能结果。这种强大的模拟方法有助于了解风险，并在面临不确定性时做出更明智的选择。

用户可以通过以下方式使用指南针：

  • 从指南针开始页面中直接创建模拟，或者通过故事中的表格创建模拟。
  • 在模拟中创建私有方案，作为回答业务问题的实验。
  • 在私有方案中，从可用动因中进行选择，然后通过设置动因的输入筛选器和随机性来配置动因。具体而言，可以在动因上设置值范围和概率分布类型，以定义其随机性。

 

 

  • 通过配置受限动因的特定筛选器从基础动因添加受限动因，并在受限动因上设置值范围和分布类型，以定义其随机性。

 

  • 之后在不同的精度模式下运行私有方案。

 

  • 根据模拟结果图，分析模拟结果的概率分布。还可以移动滑块，以了解实现低于或高于特定目标值的可能性。执行其他操作，例如定义案例设置、添加比较方案，可以深入了解模拟结果。

  

 

 

 

  • 保存后，可以通过发布私有方案来创建公用方案，使其可供有权限访问模拟的用户使用。

           

 

 

优化故事体验中新增 "onAfterDataEntryProcess" 表事件

本次更新中新增了 onAfterDataEntryProcess 表事件，用户在故事脚本中编写自定义计划流程，该事件在数据输入、复制粘贴或通过 submitData() API 更改表中的数据后触发，并且可以提供变化单元格的详细信息, 包括旧值和新值，以及上下文信息。一个常见的应用是使用这个功能完成用户输入数据的数值验证，即如果用户录入表格的数据不符合事件中的要求，则弹出警告或拒绝录入。

 

 

 

本文仅提供 SAP 分析云的最新功能和改进功能的高级概述。如果您对本博文的内容有任何疑问，可以在下方发表评论。如果您有具体的产品或技术问题，请在我们的中文 SAP Analytics Cloud 问答论坛上提问。访问我们的 SAP Analytics Cloud 社区页面，了解更多产品信息、卓越实践等。查看我们的 SAP 路线图浏览器，了解 SAP 分析云即将推出的更多功能。

entry.title: Unlocking the Power of Version Management in SAP Analytics Cloud for Planning
entry.content:
Version Management within SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) for Planning is an area that needs to be organized and pristine.  This blog explores the differences between Private and Public versions and how to manage versions effectively using examples from best-practice business content, such as the Rapid FP&A Suite for SAP S/4HANA.

Understanding Private and Public Versions in SAP Analytics Cloud

Private Versions and Public Versions serve distinct purposes in SAP Analytics Cloud:

  • Private Versions are individual workspaces where planners can experiment with scenarios and updates without affecting others. Private Versions allow users to test different planning approaches in isolation.

  • Public Versions are shared, organization-wide Versions that serve as the official source of truth for FP&A data. When a planner inputs data into a Public Version, their inputs are captured in a Private Version. The planner must then actively choose to "publish" their changes so that they appear in the Public Version.

Establish a Single Planning Version

The process within the Rapid FP&A Suite uses a single, write-enabled, public PLAN version—the Working Plan—for all active planning activities. This approach minimizes confusion by avoiding multiple editable Plan Versions simultaneously. Multiple editable Versions (e.g., Plan_1, Plan_2) upfront, can:

  • Confuse planners about where to submit their data.

  • Require constant updates to input forms and associated security settings for each Version.

  • Introduce excessive variability into workflows, leading to errors.

To simplify the process, the SAP consultants behind Rapid FP&A suggest:

  • Focus on a Single PLAN Version: Using a single PLAN Version ensures planners always know where to input their data. This reduces confusion and eliminates the administrative burden of maintaining multiple editable Versions.

  • Pre-Configured Input Forms in Rapid FP&A: Input forms in the Rapid FP&A Suite are pre-configured to filter on the PLAN Version, ensuring planners work in the "Working Plan" Version.

  • Leverage Data Actions: The Rapid FP&A Suite includes pre-configured Data Actions that allow you to copy any PLAN data into finalized, version-specific names like Target2025 or Budget2024. Once copied, these versions become read-only and are naturally referred to by their specific names (e.g., Forecast2025) for clarity and consistency.

Why does this approach work? It focuses planners on completing one version of the Plan before moving to another, avoiding the complexity and errors of managing multiple editable versions simultaneously.

Managing Data Within Private Versions

Private Versions offer a flexible environment to refine and validate planning scenarios before publishing to Public Versions. Here are some best practices:

How to Use Private Versions Effectively

Planners must flow their plan inputs through Private Versions, regardless of how Public Versions are managed, so educating Planners on how to use Private Versions optimally is crucial. Proper use of Private Versions ensures accurate updates to the plan and provides  flexibility:

  • Experiment and Refine: Use Private Versions to model scenarios, refine your inputs, and validate changes without impacting others.

  • Publish with Purpose: Once all updates are finalized, publish the changes to the Public Version to make them available for organizational use. Under the Rapid FP&A Suite, published updates flow into the PLAN Version (Working Plan), which admins can later archive into specific Version names (e.g., Budget2026) once all plans are collected.

  • Utilize Tools for Confidence: Features like the “Revert” function and “Version History Panel” provide a safety net to undo mistakes during refinement of your Private version. Remember: Reverting is unavailable after publishing it in the Public Version.

Tip: The Rapid FP&A Suite includes pre-configured input forms and sample data, allowing you to practice updating and publishing Private Versions in a "practice" environment.

Best Practices for Managing Public Versions

Creating multiple specific Version names (e.g., Budget2026, Forecast2025) at the start of every planning cycle can be problematic. This approach requires updating all input forms and reports to align with each new Version name, creating inefficiencies and potential confusion. Additionally, frequent changes to Version names can leave users unsure where to input their plan data, causing unnecessary delays and errors.

Use Categories to Organize Your Versions

You can use the category property to organize your versions by type. Categories help distinguish versions and streamline management. The following categories can help organize versions:

  • Actual, Budget, Plan, Forecast, Rolling Forecast

A model can have one public Actuals version, typically updated via import from a data source like S/4HANA. However, private copies of this version can be created for analysis or testing. Categories like Forecast allow you to create multiple forecast versions to explore optimistic, baseline, or worst-case outcomes.

Under Best Practices in the Rapid FP&A Suite, planners will focus on one scenario at a time (e.g., baseline) within the PLAN Version. Once the scenario is finalized, it can be archived under a version-specific name such as Baseline2025 or Optimistic2025, and then the planner can move on to the following scenario. This approach ensures planners complete one scenario before moving to another, avoiding confusion and streamlining the process.

Hold Actual Data in a Read-Only Version

  • Store finalized Actual data in a read-only ACTUAL Version. This ensures the integrity of historical data and provides a clear baseline for variance analysis.

Archive Specific Versions for Reporting

  • Instead of constantly updating input forms and security for new write-enabled Versions, take snapshots of the working PLAN Version at key points in the planning cycle. Archive these snapshots into finalized, read-only Versions (e.g., Budget2026, Forecast2025) using pre-built Data Actions from the Rapid FP&A Suite.

  • This approach preserves the integrity of snapshot data while avoiding the repetitive task of reconfiguring templates and security for every user or group needing write access to a plan version.

How Rapid FP&A Suite Supports Version Management

The Rapid FP&A Suite for SAP S/4HANA is a valuable toolset for:

  1. Testing and Experimentation

    • Use sample data to explore how updates to Private Versions flow into Public Versions.

  2. Streamlined Workflows

    • Discover how we maintain a single write-enabled PLAN Version for all planning activities.

    • See how admins can create finalized, locked-down Versions efficiently by copying a version from PLAN

  3. Reducing Administrative Overhead

    • You will see how pre-configured input forms align with the active working PLAN Version.

    • With only one active PLAN version all the time, you simplify security and configuration management

  4. Preserving Data Integrity

    • Finalized Versions like Budget2026 or Forecast2025 are clearly labeled and locked for accurate reporting.

    • Any other interim snapshots of PLAN can be archived for reviews or status meetings.

Benefits for FP&A Teams?

  • Planners: Clarity on where to input data with a single active Version.

  • Admins: Efficiency in managing Versions with reduced maintenance effort.

  • Stakeholders: Reliable, finalized Versions ready for variance analysis and decision-making.

Getting Started

Ready to unlock the full potential of Version Management? Leverage the Rapid FP&A Suite for SAP S/4HANA as a toolset to test and refine your planning processes. Explore its pre-configured content, including input forms, data actions, and sample scenarios, to simplify and standardize your workflows. The best part? You can download the suite in minutes and review these processes using sample data—no integration is required during your discovery phase.

How do you Download the free Rapid FP&A Suite?
Downloading the Rapid FP&A Suite is straightforward and hassle-free.
Follow these steps to get started:
1. Log in to your SAP Analytics Cloud account.   Please don't use a production tenant.
2. Navigate to the "Content Network" on the left-hand side panel of SAC
3. Select "Business Content"
4. Search for "Rapid" in the upper right-hand 'Search' box - you should see Rapid Finance, Workforce, Sales
4. Follow the instructions to complete the download and installation of each of the Rapid modules (Rapid Finance, Rapid Sales, Rapid Workforce).

Let’s make planning Rapid, Simple, and Standard with SAP Analytics Cloud!

entry.title: Details of SAP Data and Analytics Advisory Methodology Phase IV (Data Governance & Roadmaps)
entry.content:
In the previous blogs, I have provided details of the first three phases of the methodology for developing a target architecture for data-driven business outcomes:

Phase I: Scoping & Baseline Analysis
Phase II: Business Outcomes & Solution Requirements
Phase III: Capability Map & Solution Architecture

This blog is about the final phase, with focus on data governance and roadmap planning.

Overview of phase IV

Phase IV concludes your exploration of a new target architecture by assessing your data governance and organizational aspects and creating a roadmap or project plan for implementing the new architecture.

In the first two steps of this phase, you investigate relevant data governance and organizational aspects by applying the “Data and Analytics Maturity Assessment”. This topic is important as it is a key enabler to successfully establish the chosen architecture und realize the business outcomes defined in phase II.

Step 3 of this phase describes how to realize the data & analytics solution architecture by consolidating and planning all required activities and creating a high-level roadmap or project plan.

Steps IV.1 & 2: Data Governance / Roles & Organization

I have already discussed the details of the “Data and Analytics Maturity Assessment” in the previous blog. It is used to assess the maturity of data governance (step IV.1) and organizational aspects (step IV.2) and to derive the necessary improvement actions from the gaps between the current and desired maturity levels.

The activities identified during the assessment are consolidated and made available for the final step, roadmap planning.

Here is an example:

Step IV.3: Roadmaps/Project Plan

A roadmap is a strategic planning tool designed to align short-term and long-term goals with specific IT solutions. It presents these plans in formats, such as a sunray diagram or a planning chart.

An architecture roadmap organizes activities based on business needs and priorities to achieve the target architecture within a defined time frame. These activities are grouped into functional clusters to streamline planning and execution.

You start by collecting the activities to realize the defined solution architecture in an activity catalog. Each activity is defined with a name, description, and owner. Next, you review the recommended actions from the Data & Analytics Maturity Assessment, break them down into more detailed activities if necessary and add them into the activity catalog.

Then, you organize the activities according to logical clusters. You can define clusters by grouping activities that belong to the same context.

Examples of such clusters include:

  • Architecture Validation (PoC)
  • Implementation
  • Operations
  • Security
  • Know-How Transfer
  • Organization and Governance

Next, you order the activities and identify dependencies to other activities. Finally, you estimate a high-level timeline.

Here is an example of an activity catalogue:

The roadmap is then created based on the activity catalog. The following example shows the roadmap in a sunray diagram.

This completes the Data & Analytics Advisory Methodology. The next step could be the preparation of an implementation project / program where the roadmap is thoroughly planned, resources are assigned, and the architecture design is detailed out.

This article concludes the blog series that introduced the methodology and detailed the phases and steps. But this is not the end,…

…as the methodology is now available in the SAP Help Portal. It provides a comprehensive guidance on how to apply the methodology and includes the content and templates that are provided in the SAP Workzone.

We appreciate any feedback on the methodology and its documentation. We are especially interested in hearing about your experiences using the methodology. Please contact us by e-mail at sap-data-analytics-methodology@sap.com.

entry.title: SAP Analytics Cloud tenants on Quarterly Release Cycle (QRC) updates in 2025
entry.content:
Upcoming enhancements in SAP Analytics Cloud for the year 2025 can be accessed through the Road Map page.

Explore the latest features, highlights and updates from our recent releases on the page  https://www.sap.com/products/cloud-analytics/features/release-highlights.html .

If you're seeking information on release dates from the preceding year, 2024, you can refer to the details provided in this Blog Post.

SAP Analytics Cloud aligns with SAP’s global strategy for cloud application releases, following SAP Cloud products 2025 calendar:

  • SAP Analytics Cloud
  • SAP Ariba
  • SAP Business Network (incl. SAP Business Network for Procurement, SAP Business Network Asset Collaboration, and SAP Business Network for Logistics)
  • SAP BTP, ABAP environment
  • SAP Business Technology Platform (see exceptions)
  • SAP Concur
  • SAP Customer Experience* (see exceptions)
  • SAP Enterprise Asset Management
  • SAP Fieldglass
  • SAP Integrated Business Planning
  • SAP Manufacturing
  • SAP Product Lifecycle Management
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud (Public Edition) (see exceptions)
  • SAP SuccessFactors (see exceptions)

*Excludes SAP Commerce Cloud releases

Customer-impacting releases may only take place in February, May, August and November each year.

 

Following each update, upon logging into SAP Analytics Cloud, you will encounter a prominent notification banner featuring a link to our informative "What’s New" help content. You will see Help page of What’s New-2024 Releases.

 

SAP Harmonized Quarterly Updates for 2025 are the following:

  • 2025 Q1: Feb 14-16 (2025.01)
  • 2025 Q2: May 16-18 (2025.08)
  • 2025 Q3: Aug 15-17 (2025.14)
  • 2025 Q4: Nov 14-16 (2025.21)

For more details, see KBA 2888562.

SAP Analytics Cloud QRC release calendar 2025

 

SAP Analytics Cloud, as all cloud products, may have an optional, no-impact release on harmonized monthly feature delivery weekends.

As with the quarterly releases, each product has the option to release during any or all of these windows. Customers should not need to prepare for or take action for the monthly feature deliveries.

 

Note: Content can only be moved between systems on different release cycles at the time of a quarterly release cycle update.

To transport using Content Network: The source and destination systems need to be on the same quarterly version of SAP Analytics Cloud, or the following quarterly version. If the importing system is on an older version, the package won’t appear. If it’s two or more versions ahead of the exporting system, the package’s content may not be supported.

Using the Transport (file) Method (zipped / .TGZ files) – Content can only be imported in the same version of the application from which it was exported, or the subsequent version. After your system is upgraded again, you cannot import the content.

entry.title: Dashboard Design - Science & Standards, Not Random Charts & Art. (Part One)
entry.content:
• Part One - Context
  • Part Two - Chart Types
  • Part Three - Standardisation
  • Part Four - Putting it all Together

"I went to the casino yesterday and today I have €25,000 in my account"

Excellent result, or is it? Every day, I observe the content our customers create and notice a prevalent issue in a lot of dashboard designs - context. What does this mean? Simply put, a number on a dashboard remains just that - a number. Is it an improvement from last month, does it surpass the target, or is it below our expectations?

With no context there is no insight and if I had £1 for every time I seen a dashboard showing nothing more than a plain number I would retire. What does a number tell you without context - nothing, but we continue to create content that does not include context, and we think this is good design. Let's take the above example and look at some visualisations for this data:

Chart #1 provides a basic view of the balance without much additional insight beyond the figure. While it might suffice for those familiar with the expected daily balance, in practice, this lacks practicality and can be deemed as poorly designed. Contrastingly, Charts #2 and #3 offer a fresh perspective on the balance, especially when considered alongside the budget details outlined in Table #4.

The importance of context is highlighted here - each chart's value is magnified when accompanied by relevant information. Contextual clarity within dashboards is crucial for effective decision-making, as mere numbers without context may not serve their intended purpose. Let's look at another example:

This excerpt presents a segment from a Sales Dashboard (e-fashion for those that know ) indicating robust sales nearing €22 million, with a detailed breakdown of individual products illustrated in the accompanying bar chart. The overall performance appears strong, particularly highlighting Trousers as one of the top-selling items, generating €3.3 million in sales. However, consider this: what if I informed you that trouser sales were, in fact, the least successful product in our store? Would you find that plausible?

It is a straightforward reality that the dashboard does not provide a clear indication of the company's actual performance. While the numerical data is visible, it lacks significance without the necessary context. Let us examine the same data once more, this time accompanied by additional information.

 The narrative is not as favourable as it initially appears, with sales amounting to €22 million, falling short of the target of €23.4 million by nearly €1.4 million. The situation is further exacerbated by the fact that trousers experienced the most significant shortfall, missing their targets by 25% compared to other products. While the data remains unchanged, its lack of context renders it meaningless and potentially misleading.

Accurate presentation of information—including the appropriate chart type, orientation, colours, standards, and context - is essential for deriving meaningful insights from data. In this series of blogs, we will explore each of these elements in greater detail.

It is important to acknowledge that this challenge is one faced by all organizations, even SAP. The following example is based on some SAP Analytics Cloud business content that has recently been overhauled. This earlier version highlights that we too can sometimes display data in a less-than-optimal format.

 Yes, there is a little context in the right-hand panel, but the 68,299.63 kWh and 347.82 kWh numbers really tell us very little. I for one don't know if 68,299.63 kWh is high or low and do you know if 43.01 kWh is good for Hourly Max Gas Consumption... you don't? me neither.

It's not just a lack of contextual comparison that can cause problems. Scale is another that we'll cover in more detail in later offerings but check out this great find by Rob Waller (check him out on LinkedIn) regarding scale and information insight.

 Source: The Guardian Newspaper - 27th January 2025

The first chart illustrates the significant decline in the market following the news about DeepSeek. In contrast, the second chart reveals that this 'fall' was relatively minor in the grand scheme of things. The context is provided by the scale of the charts; in the first instance, the axis ranges from 19,200 to 20,000, representing only a 5% variation across the entire chart. Conversely, the second chart spans from 14,000 to 20,000, which constitutes a 30% variation, thereby offering a more precise representation of the situation.

Dashboards that lack context merely present data; they do not provide meaningful insights. SAP Analytics Cloud addresses this limitation by employing techniques such as Variances. Users can generate variances based on different versions, such as Target versus Actual or by timeframes, like Current versus Previous Period. These variances offer the insights that raw data alone cannot provide.

The example below illustrates a comparison between our actual sales figures and the target, highlighting the difference (Δ) and utilizing colour coding (to be discussed in a future blog) to indicate performance, whether positive or negative. Only two colours, that's Red and Green are acceptable, but we'll talk about that in the next posting.

We invite you to deepen your understanding through our upcoming events. Andrew and I are pleased to announce that we will be conducting four public visualization webinars in 2025. Registration links will be made available below and will activate after February 12th. We encourage you to check this page regularly for updates. Please be aware that these links will enable you to indicate your interest in participating, and we will notify you of your acceptance status once registration is open

Throw your questions and comments below and I'll always attempt to answer them as soon as I can.

Daniel

 Find me on LinkedIn

entry.title: Enhancing Filter Visibility in SAP Analytics Cloud Numeric Pointer Charts
entry.content:
Solution

Add a Text Box to the Story Page: -

You can add this text box to sit on top of the Numeric point chart, you can place it in any position on the story or drag it on top of the chart, as shown below I have placed the Text Box on top of the 2nd Numeric Point Chart: -

Click on the … button, then click on +Add -> Dynamic Text as shown below: -

A dialog box will open called: Insert Dynamic Text. In this box, scroll down and select the required chart that you want to add the Year value to.
Note: You must be careful in identifying the correct Numeric Point chart such that the correct year filter value will be displayed on each respective Numeric Point chart.
Process is shown below: -

a) Here I have selected the 2nd Numeric Point Chart: Operation, because I want to refer to the year value of only this chart.

b) Drill down on the filter section of the chart

c) Select the required filter value, here it is Plan Year.

d) Once you are done with the above, click on Create in Insert Dynamic text box.


The Year value will now start showing on the text box now: -

Note: You can also add some text manually (before & after to the year value) if you so require. Just click on the year value and position your cursor as needed.

For formatting the value in the text box, since this is dynamic text, just select the value and then right click the value in the text box and then select Edit Styling… as shown below: -

You can make multiple filter selections(e.g., on Year) on the Numeric Point Chart and then based on that the Text Box value will change automatically.

entry.title: SAC | Passing URL Parameters from Optimized Story to Data Analyzer
entry.content:
Introduction

In this blog, we explore how to seamlessly pass URL parameters from an SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) Optimized Story to the SAC Data Analyzer. This integration enhances data exploration capabilities by allowing users to drill down into data at deeper levels based on their specific requirements. Moreover, it enables the SAC Data Analyzer to open in the same state as the story, preserving filters and variables, and thus providing a more fluid and personalized data analysis experience.

Blog Highlights

This blog focuses on:

  • Writing script to pass a user’s selection from an SAC story to the Data Analyzer.
  • Utilizing SAC’s scripting classes to achieve parameter passing.
  • Demonstrating practical use cases for enhanced data exploration.

Benefits of Passing URL Parameters

  1. Enhanced Data Exploration: Users can drill down into data effortlessly, enhancing their ability to uncover insights.
  2. Advanced Features Utilization: The Data Analyzer offers various data exploration features that are readily accessible.
  3. State Preservation: By passing filters and variables, the Data Analyzer can open in the same state as the story, aligning with user needs and context.

Key Scripting Classes

To achieve the parameter passing, we utilize the following scripting classes:

  • NavigationUtils: Facilitates navigation and opening of the Data Analyzer.
  • URLParameter: Handles the creation and management of URL parameters.

Opening Data Analyzer from the Optimized Story

To open the Data Analyzer from an optimized story, the following scripting classes play a crucial role:

Example Scenario

Suppose a user wants to open the Data Analyzer by clicking on a table within the SAC story. The steps are as follows:

  1. Script on "Onclick" Function: Write the script for the table's Onclick function.
  2. Call NavigationUtils Class: Utilize this class to facilitate navigation.

 

URL Parameters

 

Creating URL parameters is essential for passing filters and variables. The URLParameter class is used for this purpose. Parameters to be specified include:

  • parameterName: Defines the parameter name to be passed, using specific naming patterns for filters and variables:
    • Filters: Use the pattern f<number>Dim (e.g., f01Dim for filter 1).
    • Variables: Use the pattern v<number>Par (e.g., v01Par for variable 1).
  • parameterValue: Corresponds to filter and variable values, matching the number in parameterName.

Example Code Snippet

 

 

NavigationUtils.openDataAnalyzer("<Connection Name>",  
                                 "<DataSource Name>",  
                                 [  
    UrlParameter.create("f01Dim","<Dimension Name>"), // filter 1  
    UrlParameter.create("f01Val","<Filter Value>"),   // filter 1 value  
    UrlParameter.create("f02Dim","<Dimension Name>"), // filter 2  
    UrlParameter.create("f02Val","<Filter Value>"),   // filter 2 value  
    UrlParameter.create("v01Par","<Variable ID>"),    // variable 1  
    UrlParameter.create("v01Val","<Variable Value>")  // variable 1 value  
                                 ],  
                                 true);

 

 

Parameters Explained

 

  • Connection: Specify the connection ID as a string.
  • dataSourceName: Provide the data source name, such as a Query name in case of BEx.
  • Parameters: Define URL parameters, including filters and variables.
  • newTab: Set to true to open in a new tab, or false to open in the same tab.

After running the story the URL created after the clicking the table will look like :

 

 

https://<tenant>#/dataanalyzer&/dz/?view_id=dataAnalyzer&connection=<Connection Name>&dataSourceName=<DataSource Name>&f01Dim=<Dimension Name>&f01Val=<Filter Value>&f02Dim=<Dimension Name>&f02Val=<Filter Value>&v01Par=<Variable ID>  &v01Val=<Variable Value>

 

 

Understanding URL Parameters

URL parameters in SAC are used to pass specific filters and variables from an Optimized Story to the Data Analyzer. This ensures that the Data Analyzer opens in the same state as the story, maintaining the user's context and selections.

Key URL Parameter Patterns

  • Filters:
    • Pattern: f<number>Dim and f<number>Val
    • Example:
      • f01Dim for the first filter dimension
      • f01Val for the first filter value
  • Variables:
    • Pattern: v<number>Par and v<number>Val
    • Example:
      • v01Par for the first variable parameter
      • v01Val for the first variable value

For more information regarding URL Parameters visit the following link :

SAP Help Portal | URL Parameters 

Conclusion

Passing URL parameters from an SAC Optimized Story to the Data Analyzer significantly enhances data exploration and user experience. By preserving the state of filters and variables, users can continue their analysis seamlessly. This scripting approach, leveraging SAC's powerful scripting classes, facilitates effective navigation and interaction between SAC components, empowering users to derive deeper insights.

If you are here, thank you so much for taking out your time to read this. Your feedback will be highly appreciated.

Thanks,
Himanshu

entry.title: Using "onAfterDataEntryProcess" event to implement Default Logic
entry.content:
A new table event “onAfterDataEntryProcess()” will be available with QRC1 2025 release in SAP Analytics Cloud. Please read this blog by my SAP colleague for details.
The “onAfterDataEntryProcess” event in SAP Analyti... - SAP 

 

We will be utilizing some simple scripting with a story using Optimized Design Experience. If you are not familiar with scripting, please check out these links.
https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/unifying-story-and-analytic-application-with-the-new-optimized-story/ba-p/13570630

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_ANALYTICS_CLOUD/00f68c2e08b941f081002fd3691d86a7/6a4db9a9c8634bcb86cecbf1f1dbbf8e.html

SAC Default Logic design flow

Detailed Steps to Implement

1. Create a new story using Optimized Design Experience

2. Add a table and layout rows, columns and filters as required to support planning entry

a. Change Id to T_Plan_Input

3. Add a button with description Clear Logs

a. Change Id to B_Clear

4. Add a button with description Calc Cost Centers #0

a. Change Id to B_DA_Run

5. Add a drop-down selection box

a. Change Id to DD_Cost_Centers

6. Add a Script Variable (under scripting)

a. Name: Changed_Cost_Centers | Type: string | Set as Array: On

7. Add a Data Action (under scripting)

a. Change Id: DA_RUN
b. Set default Version as required
c. Leave Cost Centers parameters blank

Sample layout of story

8. Edit onAfterDataEntryProcess script of T_Plan_Input table and add this code

If user makes changes to the same Cost Center in different rows, it will be added to the array only one time.
Line 8 captures the Cost Center Id only which the user can see using the Drop Down.

9. Edit onClick script of B_DA_Run button and add this code

10. Edit onClick script of B_Clear button and add this code

11. Save and close the story.

 

Configuration of Data Action Parameter

Data action using Parameter

Testing Story

1. Open Story

2. Make changes in rows for different Cost Centers as highlighted.

You will notice the button description has changed, now showing “Calc Cost Centers #2”
If you select the drop down, you can see a list of the Cost Center Id’s that have changed data.

3. Press the “Calc” button to execute the data action for only these two Cost Center’s.

The Completed message will show the number of Cost Centers that were calculated.

4. From the System menu, select Job Monitor and select the data action that was executed.

You will see the parameters that were passed to the data action for calculation.
The Cost Center description is shown in this window not the Cost Center Id.

Clear Logs

If the user wants to reset the change tracking for a planning session, they can

  1. Revert Data and
  2. Press the Clear Logs button, this will clear the global array.
